title: On-policy training
description: Learning from examples or trajectories produced by the model or agent policy that is currently being updated.
openingSummary: On-policy training means learning from examples or trajectories that the current model or agent policy produces right now, rather than from a fixed dataset collected earlier or from behavior that no longer matches the weights being updated.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: On-policy training updates a model or agent using data that the current policy generates during training. Each refresh samples answers, actions, or full trajectories from the weights you are about to change. The optimizer then learns from that fresh behavior instead of treating an older snapshot of the model as if it were still in charge.

whyItMatters.title: Why It Matters
whyItMatters.body: Language models and agents change their behavior as weights move. A demonstration set recorded last week, or traces from an older checkpoint, may not show the mistakes and answer styles the live policy makes today. On-policy loops try to close that gap by training on what the current policy actually does now. The tradeoff is cost and stability: refreshing samples takes compute, and weak sampling or rewards can reinforce bad habits—but the core idea is matching the update to present behavior.

simpleExample.title: Simple Example
simpleExample.body: Imagine a chat model that keeps giving overconfident math answers. A fixed correction dataset might not include the exact wrong proofs the model writes after the latest fine-tune. An on-policy loop samples fresh answers from the current weights, scores them, and updates on those new mistakes. The trainer sees the failure modes the deployed policy would show today, not only errors that appeared in an older log file.

commonConfusions.title: Common Confusions
commonConfusions.body: On-policy training is not the same as any single recipe. Reinforcement learning from human feedback, verifiable-reward training, and on-policy distillation can all use on-policy data, but on-policy names the data source—not the optimizer or loss function. It is also not automatically better than off-policy training. Off-policy methods reuse older trajectories or static datasets and can be cheaper and more stable; on-policy methods prioritize freshness. The useful question is whether the update needs behavior from the policy you are changing right now.
